The goal is to remove the \r at the end of each line. In Linux, the newline character can be represented as \n or \r. But in many cases, when you use tail -n +2, it skips the last line, but here the user wants to remove the \r at the end of each line. So, perhaps using a command like awk to process each line.

Wait, but the user wants the result as printable characters or backslash escapes. So, if the line ends with \r, replacing it with an empty string would remove it. So, the approach would be to read each line, strip the trailing \r, and then print the line. Using awk, we can do this. The command would be something like:

awk ' { print $0 }' file.txt

But wait, that would print each line as is. If the line ends with \r, then the print would include that. So perhaps using a command that removes the trailing \r. 

Alternatively, using sed. The command could be:
